1.note: James commends Job's perseverance to a suffering community: 'You have heard of the steadfastness of Job, and you have seen the purpose of the Lord, how the Lord is compassionate and merciful.' Job's endurance through undeserved suffering is the paradigm of faith-in-the-dark that James holds up as the model for trials. The 'purpose of the Lord' James mentions is the vindication of Job's integrity at the end — visible only in retrospect, which is how faith must function in the midst of suffering.

6.note: The divine council scene — 'the sons of God came to present themselves before the LORD, and Satan also came among them' — reveals the cosmic-courtroom framework of OT theology: accusation happens before God. Revelation 12:10 names the dragon as 'the accuser of our brothers...who accuses them day and night before our God.' The heavenly council in Job 1-2 is the backstory that John's apocalyptic vision resolves: what the cross achieved is precisely the expulsion of the accuser from heaven.

The adversary (haSSāṭān — the Accuser) stands before YHWH to accuse Job just as he stands at the right hand of Joshua the high priest to accuse him in Zechariah 3:1. Both scenes show the Accuser's activity as cosmic complaint before the divine throne — and in both cases, YHWH rebukes him and vindicates the accused. The pattern culminates in Christ, who silences the accuser permanently by bearing the verdict himself (Rom 8:33-34).

21.note: 'Naked I came from my mother's womb, and naked shall I return. The LORD gave, and the LORD has taken away; blessed be the name of the LORD.' Paul echoes Job's detachment: 'we brought nothing into the world, and we cannot take anything out of the world' (1 Tim 6:7), grounding contentment in the same theology — what we hold is held on loan. Job's doxology amid loss is the OT's purest expression of the posture Paul commands.

Job's 'blessed be the name of the LORD' after total loss is the OT precedent for Paul's 'I have learned, in whatever situation I am, to be content' (Phil 4:11). Both express that worship is not contingent on circumstances. The difference is that Job learns it through crisis; Paul through a lifetime of imprisonment, beatings, and shipwrecks. Both reach the same theology: the LORD's name is blessable precisely when it costs most.

The LORD gave, and the LORD has taken away; blessed be the name of the LORD — Job's response to total loss; Paul's 'I have learned, in whatever state I am, to be content' echoes the Job pattern of accepting both abundance and loss from the hand of God

1.note: The second divine council meeting reprises the first — 'Satan also came among them to present himself before the LORD.' The accuser's persistence in presenting himself before God (twice now, unbidden) is the OT picture of what John sees expelled at the cross: the accuser who 'accuses them day and night before our God' is cast down by 'the blood of the Lamb and by the word of their testimony' (Rev 12:11). The patient, repeated accusation of Job prefigures the permanent accusation that Christ's death finally answers.

10.note: Job refuses to sin against God even when his wife tells him to curse God and die: 'Shall we receive good from God, and shall we not receive evil?' The author of Hebrews frames suffering similarly: 'It is for discipline that you have to endure. God is treating you as sons' (Heb 12:7). Job doesn't have this explanation — he suffers without knowing why — yet his question implicitly grants God sovereignty over both good and evil in a way that Hebrews' theology of discipline presupposes.

Job receives evil from God without sinning — his refusal to curse is the act of faith that his wife cannot comprehend. James calls the community to the same disposition: 'Count it all joy, my brothers, when you meet trials of various kinds, for you know that the testing of your faith produces steadfastness' (Jas 1:2-3). Job is the OT demonstration that faith-tested-and-held is possible; James's exhortation presupposes that it is also commanded.

3.note: Job's desperate lament ('Let the day perish on which I was born') expresses suffering that finds no human parallel in the OT. The depth of lament — wishing for non-existence rather than continued anguish — is the OT's closest approach to Jesus in Gethsemane: 'My soul is very sorrowful, even to death' (Matt 26:38). Both are fully innocent sufferers under crushing weight; but where Job laments the day of his birth, Jesus accepts the cup and goes to the cross. The distance between them is the distance between the type and the one who exhausts suffering's deepest logic on behalf of others.

'Let the day perish on which I was born' — Job's lament is echoed almost verbatim by Jeremiah (Jer 20:14-18), creating an OT tradition of prophetic-lament that cries out from the depths of innocent suffering. Both Job and Jeremiah are figures of faithful suffering who did not get immediate answers — anticipating the one who cried 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?' (Ps 22:1; Matt 27:46) while remaining obedient to death.

17.note: Eliphaz asks: 'Can mortal man be in the right before God? Can a man be pure before his Maker?' His question assumes the answer is 'no' — and intends to convict Job of secret sin. Paul gives the same answer but for different purposes: 'by works of the law no human being will be justified in his sight' (Rom 3:20). Eliphaz uses the truth to condemn Job; Paul uses it to drive all to the righteousness that comes through faith in Christ.

33.note: There is no arbiter between us who might lay his hand on both of us — Job's longing for a mediator who can bridge the gap between the holy God and the accused human; Paul's 'there is one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus' is the direct answer to Job's longing: the mediator Job needed exists

25.note: I know that my Redeemer lives, and at the last he will stand upon the earth — Job's confession of faith in a living Redeemer who will vindicate him after death; the resurrection of Christ is the answer to Job's hope: the Redeemer who lives has stood upon the earth, and because he lives Job will live also

4.note: Where were you when I laid the foundation of the earth? — the divine speech from the whirlwind (chs. 38-41) confronts Job with the Creator's incomprehensible majesty; John 1:1 grounds Christ as the one who was present at that foundation: in the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God — the one speaking from the whirlwind and the one who became flesh are the same person
